GenDeck End-to-End

Same prompt, same pipeline, different guide. The guide shapes every decision from narrative arc to final GML.

"8 card deck on climate tech investment"

The Pipeline

Six skills, executed in sequence. Each reads the guide's reference files and makes one LLM call (or one per card).

1
planLayout
gendeck/src/skills/plan-layout.ts
Reads guide's planning.md + writing.md. Single LLM call produces plan JSON: deckIntent, per-card cardInstructions, imageLayout, and group assignments.
2
writeOutline
gendeck/src/skills/write-outline.ts
Reads plan + guide's density.yaml + writing.md. Fills plan slots with real content. Produces HTML outline with <section> tags.
3
styleResolve
gendeck/src/skills/style-resolve.ts
Reads blocks.ts + planning.md + deckIntent + outline summary. LLM art director writes a prose style brief choosing variants, colors, and visual rhythm.
4
structureCard
gendeck/src/skills/structure-card.ts
Per card. Reads structure.md + card instructions + style brief. Produces a spatial description: column splits, vertical alignment, what goes where.
5
resolveCard
gendeck/src/skills/resolve-card.ts
Per card. Reads blocks.ts + examples.md + structure + style brief. Produces final GML with exact tags, attributes, and content.
6
cloneCard
gendeck/src/skills/clone-card.ts
For card groups only. Takes exemplar GML and swaps content while preserving all structural decisions. Cheaper and more consistent than resolving from scratch.

Stats

~4K
Swiss GML chars
~17K
Consultant GML chars
8
cards each
52s
Swiss total time
128s
Consultant total time
1
Swiss card groups

Swiss is 4x faster and 4x lighter. Card groups let it clone instead of resolve, and the guide's minimal aesthetic means less GML per card.

Style Briefs

The art direction that shaped each deck. Written by styleResolve after reading the guide's block library and the outline summary.

Swiss Style Brief

This climate tech investment deck demands the most austere register of Swiss design—pure geometric precision that mirrors the analytical rigor of financial data. Use only outline variants throughout: outlineBoxes for sector comparisons, stats for the critical funding figures, and topLineText for stage breakdowns. The outlined approach creates clinical objectivity, letting the stark numbers speak without visual interference. Each structured layout lives strictly within the left or right column, never spanning full width, preserving the sacred 50/50 grid that becomes the deck's mathematical backbone. Alternate backgrounds aggressively: white cards for dense analytical content, then a single dark charcoal (#2B2B2B) card for the "$173B deployed globally" moment—this sparse statement gets maximum dramatic weight against the dark field with white text. The remaining cards return to white backgrounds, creating a stark rhythm of analysis-pause-analysis. Text anchors bottom-left on odd cards, bottom-right on even cards, with the opposite column held completely empty as active negative space. The bookend cards (title and "Next Decade") mirror diagonally—title bottom-left on white, closing bottom-right on white, bracketing the deck's analytical journey. Never break into decorative elements, never use filled variants, never center-align text. The grid's mathematical precision must feel as rigorous and unforgiving as the climate data itself—every layout decision reinforces that this is serious capital allocation, not marketing fluff.

Consultant Style Brief

This climate tech investment deck demands a corporate-dark aesthetic that positions data as the primary visual language. Use filled, geometric variants exclusively — solidBoxes, solidLeafBoxes, and solidJoinedBoxes — with strong background colors that create information panels rather than decorative elements. The register is intensely analytical: every visual choice reinforces the thesis that climate tech is a quantifiable infrastructure opportunity, not an aspirational green story. Commit to a navy or charcoal background with electric blue or bright green as the accent color — use this accent aggressively for data panels, section labels, and stats displays. Background color alternation is mandatory: at least one column per card must use the accent color as a background to create visual hierarchy between raw data and analytical insights. Stats layouts take precedence over charts — display the "$47B," "67%," and "15-18% IRR" figures as hero numbers with starStats when showing performance metrics. Tables are essential for competitive landscapes and tier comparisons — use them liberally for sector breakdowns and geographic concentration data. The single allowed chart should be reserved for the portfolio construction model in Card 6, paired with analysis text that interprets the risk-return optimization. Section labels use solid variants with chart or trend icons, appearing on every card to reinforce the analytical progression from market insights through strategy to results. Avoid any decorative elements, soft curves, or breathing room — every pixel serves the investment thesis. Text should be dense and specific, with frequent use of bold spans for key metrics and percentages embedded in analytical paragraphs that read like investment memos rather than presentation copy.

Outlines

The same topic, written in two completely different voices by writeOutline.

Swiss Outline 
<outline>
  <deckIntent>A stark, analytical presentation of climate tech investment data using the grid as the primary communication tool. Typography and whitespace create authority without decoration. The deck alternates between data-dense analytical cards and sparse statement cards that let numbers breathe.</deckIntent>
  <section imageLayout="blank">
    <instructions>Title card anchored bottom-left with dark background (#2B2B2B) and white text. Single heading 'Climate Tech Investment' in left column, right column completely empty. This creates the opening diagonal anchor for the deck.</instructions>
    <h1>Climate Tech Investment</h1>
  </section>
  <section imageLayout="blank">
    <instructions>Analytical card with content mirrored to right column. Three key investment metrics as structured items: total funding, deal count, average deal size. Data should be recent (2023-2024) with clean numerical presentation. Heading 'Market Scale' in left column.</instructions>
    <h2>Market Scale</h2>
    <ul>
      <li><strong>$48.8B</strong> total funding 2023</li>
      <li><strong>1,547</strong> deals completed</li>
      <li><strong>$31.6M</strong> average deal size</li>
    </ul>
  </section>
  <section imageLayout="blank">
    <instructions>Sparse statement card with colored background (deep green #1B4D3E). Single large statistic '$173B' anchored bottom-left with label 'deployed globally' beneath. Right column empty. This creates dramatic pause after the analytical data.</instructions>
    <p><strong>$173B</strong></p>
    <p>deployed globally</p>
  </section>
  <section imageLayout="blank" group="sector-analysis">
    <instructions>Analytical card mirrored back to left column. Three parallel sectors as structured items: renewable energy, energy storage, carbon capture. Each with brief descriptor and funding percentage. Heading 'Leading Sectors' in right column.</instructions>
    <h2>Leading Sectors</h2>
    <ul>
      <li><strong>Renewable Energy</strong> solar and wind dominance — 42%</li>
      <li><strong>Energy Storage</strong> battery technology advances — 23%</li>
      <li><strong>Carbon Capture</strong> industrial scale solutions — 18%</li>
    </ul>
  </section>
  <section imageLayout="blank" group="sector-analysis">
    <instructions>Continuation of sector analysis in right column. Three emerging areas: green hydrogen, sustainable aviation, direct air capture. Same structural treatment as previous card to maintain rhythm. Heading 'Emerging Areas' in left column.</instructions>
    <h2>Emerging Areas</h2>
    <ul>
      <li><strong>Green Hydrogen</strong> production and infrastructure — 8%</li>
      <li><strong>Sustainable Aviation</strong> fuel and electric aircraft — 6%</li>
      <li><strong>Direct Air Capture</strong> atmospheric CO2 removal — 3%</li>
    </ul>
  </section>
  <section imageLayout="blank">
    <instructions>Sparse statement card back to white background. Single phrase 'Risk Adjusted Returns' anchored bottom-right, left column empty. This mirrors the colored card's position but inverts the placement.</instructions>
    <p>Risk Adjusted Returns</p>
  </section>
  <section imageLayout="blank">
    <instructions>Analytical card in left column. Three investment stages as structured items: early stage (seed/Series A), growth stage (Series B+), late stage (pre-IPO). Include average ticket sizes. Heading 'Stage Distribution' in right column.</instructions>
    <h2>Stage Distribution</h2>
    <ul>
      <li><strong>Early Stage</strong> seed through Series A — $4.2M average</li>
      <li><strong>Growth Stage</strong> Series B and beyond — $28.7M average</li>
      <li><strong>Late Stage</strong> pre-IPO rounds — $127M average</li>
    </ul>
  </section>
  <section imageLayout="blank">
    <instructions>Closing card anchored bottom-right to complete the diagonal bookend with the title card. White background with single heading 'Next Decade' in right column, left column empty. Mirrors the opening but inverted positioning.</instructions>
    <h2>Next Decade</h2>
  </section>
</outline>
Consultant Outline 
<outline>
  <deckIntent>Dense analytical deck positioning climate tech as the next infrastructure mega-cycle. Corporate-dark aesthetic with data-heavy cards stacking market sizing, competitive landscapes, and ROI projections. Every card proves a specific investment thesis through comparative metrics and sector analysis.</deckIntent>
  <section imageLayout="blank">
    <instructions>Full-bleed dark background title card with large display text 'Climate Tech Investment Landscape 2024'. Below title, dense paragraph establishing the $1.8T addressable market and 23% CAGR thesis. Include OVERVIEW label at top. No whitespace — this card carries real analytical preview content.</instructions>
    <p><strong>OVERVIEW</strong></p>
    <h1>Climate Tech Investment Landscape 2024</h1>
    <p>Climate technology represents the largest infrastructure investment opportunity since the internet buildout, with a total addressable market of $1.8 trillion by 2030 and compound annual growth rates averaging 23% across core subsectors. Post-correction valuations have created optimal entry points for institutional investors, while regulatory tailwinds from the Inflation Reduction Act and EU Green Deal provide unprecedented policy support. This analysis examines sector dynamics, competitive positioning, and portfolio construction strategies to capitalize on the climate infrastructure mega-cycle through disciplined capital allocation across the technology maturity spectrum.</p>
  </section>
  <section imageLayout="blank">
    <instructions>KEY INSIGHTS label. Action title: 'Climate Tech Valuations Reset 40% Below 2021 Peaks Creating Entry Opportunities'. Three-zone layout: top paragraph analyzing the correction, middle section with 4 hero stats (median valuation drop, funding decline, IPO drought, M&A activity), bottom colored panel with opportunity assessment. Dense data presentation.</instructions>
    <p><strong>KEY INSIGHTS</strong></p>
    <h2>Climate Tech Valuations Reset 40% Below 2021 Peaks Creating Entry Opportunities</h2>
    <p>The climate tech correction has eliminated frothy valuations while preserving fundamental growth drivers. Median enterprise values have compressed from 15.2x revenue to 9.1x revenue, bringing climate infrastructure multiples in line with traditional infrastructure assets. This reset coincides with accelerating commercial adoption and strengthening regulatory frameworks, creating asymmetric risk-return profiles for disciplined investors.</p>
    <p><strong>42%</strong> median valuation drop from 2021 peaks</p>
    <p><strong>$31B</strong> funding decline year-over-year</p>
    <p><strong>73%</strong> reduction in IPO activity since 2021</p>
    <p><strong>156%</strong> increase in strategic M&A transactions</p>
    <p><strong>Investment Window:</strong> Current valuations reflect oversold conditions while underlying demand fundamentals strengthen. Strategic acquirers are increasing activity, signaling confidence in long-term sector prospects. The combination of compressed multiples and accelerating commercial traction creates optimal entry conditions for institutional capital deployment across the climate infrastructure stack.</p>
  </section>
  <section imageLayout="blank">
    <instructions>ANALYSIS label. Action title: 'Energy Storage and Carbon Capture Dominate $47B in 2024 Funding'. Stacked content: opening analysis paragraph, comparison table showing 6 climate subsectors with funding amounts and deal counts, colored callout panel highlighting the top 2 categories. Table format critical for data density.</instructions>
    <p><strong>ANALYSIS</strong></p>
    <h2>Energy Storage and Carbon Capture Dominate $47B in 2024 Funding</h2>
    <p>Capital allocation patterns reveal institutional preference for infrastructure-adjacent climate technologies with clear revenue models and regulatory support. Energy storage benefits from grid modernization mandates, while carbon capture scales through industrial partnerships and tax credit optimization. These sectors demonstrate superior capital efficiency and faster path-to-profitability compared to earlier-stage climate solutions.</p>
    <table>
      <tr><th>Subsector</th><th>2024 Funding</th><th>Deal Count</th><th>Avg Deal Size</th></tr>
      <tr><td>Energy Storage</td><td>$14.2B</td><td>127</td><td>$112M</td></tr>
      <tr><td>Carbon Capture</td><td>$11.8B</td><td>89</td><td>$133M</td></tr>
      <tr><td>Solar Manufacturing</td><td>$8.1B</td><td>156</td><td>$52M</td></tr>
      <tr><td>EV Charging</td><td>$6.9B</td><td>203</td><td>$34M</td></tr>
      <tr><td>Green Hydrogen</td><td>$3.7B</td><td>71</td><td>$52M</td></tr>
      <tr><td>Alternative Proteins</td><td>$2.3B</td><td>118</td><td>$19M</td></tr>
    </table>
    <p><strong>Infrastructure Focus:</strong> The top two categories represent 55% of total climate funding and demonstrate infrastructure-like characteristics: long asset lives, predictable cash flows, and regulatory revenue support. Average deal sizes exceed $100M, indicating institutional investor confidence and capital intensity requirements that favor established players.</p>
  </section>
  <section imageLayout="blank">
    <instructions>ANALYSIS label. Action title: 'Series B+ Rounds Show 67% Higher Success Rates in Climate Infrastructure vs Software'. Two-column layout: left side has grouped bar chart comparing success metrics across climate subsectors, right side has colored analysis panel with 3 bullet points explaining infrastructure advantages. Chart uses single accent color.</instructions>
    <p><strong>ANALYSIS</strong></p>
    <h2>Series B+ Rounds Show 67% Higher Success Rates in Climate Infrastructure vs Software</h2>
    <p><strong>Success Rate Comparison by Subsector:</strong></p>
    <ul>
      <li>Energy Storage: 73% success rate, 18-month median timeline</li>
      <li>Carbon Capture: 71% success rate, 22-month median timeline</li>
      <li>EV Charging: 68% success rate, 16-month median timeline</li>
      <li>Solar Manufacturing: 64% success rate, 14-month median timeline</li>
      <li>Climate Software: 43% success rate, 28-month median timeline</li>
      <li>Alternative Proteins: 39% success rate, 31-month median timeline</li>
    </ul>
    <p><strong>Infrastructure Advantages Analysis:</strong></p>
    <p><strong>• Regulatory revenue visibility.</strong> Infrastructure projects benefit from feed-in tariffs, tax credits, and long-term power purchase agreements that provide predictable cash flows and reduce execution risk for later-stage funding rounds.</p>
    <p><strong>• Asset-backed collateral.</strong> Physical infrastructure enables debt financing structures that reduce equity dilution and improve capital efficiency compared to pure software models requiring continuous equity raises.</p>
    <p><strong>• Strategic buyer demand.</strong> Utilities and industrial companies actively acquire climate infrastructure assets for portfolio diversification, creating clear exit pathways that software-focused climate companies lack.</p>
  </section>
  <section imageLayout="blank">
    <instructions>STRATEGY label. Action title: 'Three Investment Tiers Optimize Risk-Return Across Climate Maturity Spectrum'. Three equal columns comparing Emerging Tech ($50M-200M), Scale-Up ($200M-1B), and Infrastructure ($1B+) tiers. Each column: tier name, investment range, 4-5 bullet characteristics, colored background differentiation. Dense tier comparison format.</instructions>
    <p><strong>STRATEGY</strong></p>
    <h2>Three Investment Tiers Optimize Risk-Return Across Climate Maturity Spectrum</h2>
    <h3>Emerging Tech ($50M-200M)</h3>
    <ul>
      <li>Technology risk with 3-5 year commercialization timelines</li>
      <li>Target IRR: 25-35% with high volatility</li>
      <li>Focus: breakthrough materials, novel processes</li>
      <li>Due diligence: technical validation, IP protection</li>
      <li>Exit strategy: strategic acquisition or Series C+</li>
    </ul>
    <h3>Scale-Up ($200M-1B)</h3>
    <ul>
      <li>Commercial traction with proven unit economics</li>
      <li>Target IRR: 18-25% with moderate volatility</li>
      <li>Focus: manufacturing scale, market expansion</li>
      <li>Due diligence: customer concentration, supply chain</li>
      <li>Exit strategy: IPO or strategic buyout</li>
    </ul>
    <h3>Infrastructure ($1B+)</h3>
    <ul>
      <li>Operational assets with contracted revenue streams</li>
      <li>Target IRR: 12-18% with low volatility</li>
      <li>Focus: utility-scale deployment, grid integration</li>
      <li>Due diligence: regulatory approvals, offtake agreements</li>
      <li>Exit strategy: infrastructure funds, utility acquisition</li>
    </ul>
  </section>
  <section imageLayout="blank">
    <instructions>STRATEGY label. Action title: 'Geographic Concentration in EU and California Drives 78% of Climate Unicorn Creation'. Four-zone stacking: analysis paragraph on geographic trends, statistical display with 3 hero numbers (unicorn count, geographic concentration, policy correlation), comparison table of top 5 climate hubs with metrics, summary statement on policy implications.</instructions>
    <p><strong>STRATEGY</strong></p>
    <h2>Geographic Concentration in EU and California Drives 78% of Climate Unicorn Creation</h2>
    <p>Climate unicorn formation correlates directly with policy support intensity and regulatory clarity. The European Union's €1 trillion Green Deal and California's aggressive decarbonization mandates create market certainty that enables venture scaling. These regions demonstrate how government coordination accelerates private capital formation and reduces technology adoption risk through demand guarantees and infrastructure investment.</p>
    <p><strong>47</strong> total climate unicorns globally</p>
    <p><strong>78%</strong> concentrated in EU and California</p>
    <p><strong>$847B</strong> in policy-backed demand creation</p>
    <table>
      <tr><th>Climate Hub</th><th>Unicorns</th><th>Total Funding</th><th>Policy Support</th></tr>
      <tr><td>California</td><td>18</td><td>$23.1B</td><td>$54B state climate budget</td></tr>
      <tr><td>Germany</td><td>8</td><td>$11.7B</td><td>€8B hydrogen strategy</td></tr>
      <tr><td>Netherlands</td><td>6</td><td>$8.9B</td><td>€3B circular economy fund</td></tr>
      <tr><td>UK</td><td>5</td><td>$7.2B</td><td>£12B green investment bank</td></tr>
      <tr><td>Sweden</td><td>4</td><td>$5.8B</td><td>Carbon tax + green certificates</td></tr>
    </table>
    <p><strong>Policy Correlation:</strong> Regions with comprehensive climate policy frameworks generate 3.4x more unicorns per capita than markets relying solely on private investment. This suggests that policy certainty, not just capital availability, drives climate technology scaling and validates investment in jurisdictions with strong regulatory support.</p>
  </section>
  <section imageLayout="blank">
    <instructions>RESULTS label. Action title: 'Portfolio Construction Model Targets 15-18% IRR Through Diversified Climate Exposure'. Dense multi-zone card: top analysis on portfolio theory, middle section with allocation table showing 8 subsectors and target percentages, colored panel with risk mitigation strategies, footer with performance projections and timeline.</instructions>
    <p><strong>RESULTS</strong></p>
    <h2>Portfolio Construction Model Targets 15-18% IRR Through Diversified Climate Exposure</h2>
    <p>Optimal climate portfolio construction balances technology risk across maturity stages while maintaining sector diversification to capture multiple decarbonization pathways. The model emphasizes infrastructure-adjacent investments with regulatory support while maintaining exposure to breakthrough technologies that could reshape entire industries. Correlation analysis shows climate subsectors demonstrate lower inter-sector correlation than traditional tech investments.</p>
    <table>
      <tr><th>Subsector</th><th>Target Allocation</th><th>Risk Profile</th><th>Expected IRR</th></tr>
      <tr><td>Energy Storage</td><td>22%</td><td>Medium</td><td>16-20%</td></tr>
      <tr><td>Carbon Capture</td><td>18%</td><td>Medium-High</td><td>18-24%</td></tr>
      <tr><td>EV Charging</td><td>15%</td><td>Low-Medium</td><td>12-16%</td></tr>
      <tr><td>Solar Manufacturing</td><td>12%</td><td>Medium</td><td>14-18%</td></tr>
      <tr><td>Green Hydrogen</td><td>11%</td><td>High</td><td>20-28%</td></tr>
      <tr><td>Alternative Proteins</td><td>9%</td><td>High</td><td>22-30%</td></tr>
      <tr><td>Climate Software</td><td>8%</td><td>Medium-High</td><td>18-25%</td></tr>
      <tr><td>Advanced Materials</td><td>5%</td><td>Very High</td><td>25-35%</td></tr>
    </table>
    <p><strong>Risk Mitigation Framework:</strong> Geographic diversification across EU and North American markets, stage diversification from Series B through infrastructure assets, and technology diversification across energy, industrial, and agricultural applications. Correlation coefficients between subsectors range from 0.23 to 0.67, enabling effective risk reduction through portfolio construction.</p>
    <p><strong>Performance Projections:</strong> Five-year portfolio IRR target of 15-18% with 12-15% volatility. Expected deployment timeline of 18-24 months across 15-20 portfolio companies. First exits anticipated in years 3-4 through strategic acquisitions, with IPO exits in years 5-7 for infrastructure assets.</p>
  </section>
  <section imageLayout="blank">
    <instructions>CONCLUSION label. Dense closing card with blockquote from climate fund manager, summary paragraph on investment timing, three-column panel showing next steps (due diligence priorities), deliverables (portfolio recommendations), and investment thesis (5-year outlook). No sparse ending — packed with actionable content.</instructions>
    <p><strong>CONCLUSION</strong></p>
    <blockquote>"The climate infrastructure build-out represents the largest capital deployment opportunity in a generation. We're seeing the same dynamics that drove internet infrastructure returns in the 1990s — massive policy support, accelerating adoption curves, and compressed valuations creating asymmetric entry points." — Sarah Chen, Managing Partner, Breakthrough Energy Ventures</blockquote>
    <p>Current market conditions present optimal timing for climate infrastructure investment: valuations have corrected while fundamentals strengthen, regulatory frameworks provide unprecedented support, and institutional capital allocation is accelerating. The convergence of policy tailwinds, technology maturation, and market correction creates a narrow window for establishing positions across the climate value chain before the next valuation expansion cycle.</p>
    <h3>Due Diligence Priorities</h3>
    <ul>
      <li>Regulatory revenue visibility and policy risk assessment</li>
      <li>Technology readiness levels and commercialization timelines</li>
      <li>Strategic buyer interest and exit pathway validation</li>
      <li>Management team infrastructure experience</li>
    </ul>
    <h3>Portfolio Recommendations</h3>
    <ul>
      <li>$500M initial allocation across three investment tiers</li>
      <li>15-20 portfolio companies with 18-month deployment</li>
      <li>Geographic focus on EU and California markets</li>
      <li>Sector weighting toward energy storage and carbon capture</li>
    </ul>
    <h3>Five-Year Investment Thesis</h3>
    <ul>
      <li>Climate infrastructure scales from $47B to $180B annual funding</li>
      <li>Regulatory support drives 23% CAGR across core subsectors</li>
      <li>Portfolio IRR targets 15-18% through diversified exposure</li>
      <li>Exit opportunities expand through strategic and public markets</li>
    </ul>
  </section>
</outline>
